stops.name: QV Cafe & Bakery - Zoomr Zone
stops.desc: Grab a coffee and a fresh pastry before you roll out. This is your Zoomr Zone — unlock your Zoomr in the Levy app, snap on your helmet, and meet your virtual guide. The Coastal Loop is a ~7 km mostly-flat ride hugging Victoria's waterfront.
stops.tip: Fill your water bottle here — the next café stop isn't until you loop back.

stops.name: Inner Harbour
stops.desc: The Inner Harbour is Victoria's living room: the ivy-covered Fairmont Empress, the floodlit Parliament Buildings, float planes landing on the water, and buskers along the causeway. In summer the harbour fills with kayaks and water taxis.
stops.tip: Stay on the lower causeway path — it's car-free and far more scenic than the road above.

stops.name: Fisherman's Wharf
stops.desc: Fisherman's Wharf is a riot of candy-coloured floating homes, food kiosks, and resident harbour seals that pop up looking for a snack. Originally a working fish dock, it's now one of the city's most-loved photo spots.
stops.tip: Barb's Fish & Chips is the local legend here — and yes, the seals really will wave.

stops.name: Dallas Rd Swim Pavillion
stops.desc: Dallas Road runs along open ocean with the Olympic Mountains across the strait. The swim pavilion marks a beloved cold-water swimming spot. Expect wind, driftwood beaches, and dogs everywhere on the lower trail.
stops.tip: This stretch can be breezy — it's why the kite-flyers love the clifftops above you.

stops.name: Clover Point
stops.tagline: Windswept point jutting into the strait
stops.desc: Clover Point pushes out into Juan de Fuca Strait, a favourite for kite-flying, whale-spotting, and watching cargo ships drift past. On a clear day Washington's Olympic Range looks close enough to touch.
stops.tip: Pause at the tip — grey whales and orcas are sometimes spotted offshore in spring.

stops.name: Beacon Hill Park
stops.desc: Beacon Hill Park is 200 acres of gardens, ponds, and roaming peacocks, plus a children's petting farm. Nearby stands Mile 0 of the Trans-Canada Highway and a towering totem pole. It's the green finish before you coast back to the café.
stops.tip: Mile 0 and the Terry Fox statue are a 2-minute detour — worth the photo.

legs: Welcome to the Coastal Loop! Roll out from the café heading toward the water. You'll drop gently downhill toward the Inner Harbour — keep right and enjoy the breeze.

As you approach the Inner Harbour, look for the float planes — Victoria has one of the busiest seaplane harbours in the world. The Parliament Buildings ahead are outlined by over 3,000 lights every night.
Rolling on to Fisherman's Wharf now. Fun fact: those floating homes are fully off-grid-ish little communities — some have been here for decades. Watch for seals in the water on your right.
Heading toward Dallas Road and the open ocean. You're now riding along the Strait of Juan de Fuca — that water separates Canada from the USA, and those snowy peaks are in Washington State.

On to Clover Point. This whole shoreline is part of a Pacific flyway — keep an eye out for bald eagles and, if you're lucky, a spout offshore. The point gets windy, which is exactly why kite-flyers love it.

Final stretch into Beacon Hill Park. This park has been a gathering place for thousands of years and is home to one of the world's tallest totem poles. After this, it's an easy cruise back to the café to close the loop.

qa.a: Great eats on this loop: Barb's Fish & Chips at Fisherman's Wharf, the café kiosks along the Inner Harbour causeway, and of course QV Cafe & Bakery at your start/finish. For a sit-down meal, the Inner Harbour has the most options.
Public washrooms are available at the Inner Harbour causeway, Fisherman's Wharf, and inside Beacon Hill Park near the petting farm.
Keep your eyes peeled! Harbour seals hang around Fisherman's Wharf, bald eagles soar over Dallas Road, and grey whales or orcas are occasionally spotted off Clover Point in spring and fall.

Victoria is the capital of British Columbia, on the traditional territories of the Lekwungen (Songhees and Esquimalt) peoples. The Inner Harbour's Parliament Buildings opened in 1898, and Beacon Hill Park has been a meeting place for thousands of years.
The full Coastal Loop is about 7 km and mostly flat. At a relaxed pace with photo stops, plan for 1.5 to 2 hours. Strong riders can do it in under an hour.
Victoria is one of Canada's mildest cities, but the waterfront can be windy — especially around Clover Point and Dallas Road. Bring a light layer even on sunny days.
There are Zoomr Zone racks at the Inner Harbour, Fisherman's Wharf, and Beacon Hill Park. Always lock up — and your Zoomr's GPS lock can be triggered from the app if you step away.
Quick Zoomr rules: helmets are mandatory, top speed is 25 km/h, single rider only, and you ride in bike lanes or as far right as practical — no sidewalks. Minimum age 16.
Zoomrs start and end at a Zoomr Zone hub (the pilot hub is at QV Cafe & Bakery). Lock to the rack when you're done — ending a ride outside a Zone adds fees.

tours.blurb: Victoria's greatest-hits waterfront loop — the Inner Harbour, Fisherman's Wharf, open-ocean shoreline and Beacon Hill Park. Flat, scenic and easy.

24.text: Cross the street turn left onto Wharf St. bike path

304.text: Turn right down to Clover Point

349.text: Turn left down dallas road waterfront trail

398.text: turn right to head into beacon hill park

added.text: Turn left then right into fisherman's Wharf
Bear right onto Dallas Road waterfront trail

stops.name: QV Cafe & Bakery - Zoomr Zone
stops.desc: Gear up for the longest of the three loops — about 14 km, almost entirely flat rail-trail and waterfront. Top up your water bottle here.
stops.tip: This is the long one — start with full water and a snack.

stops.name: Johnson Street Bridge
stops.desc: The Johnson Street Bridge is a bright-blue bascule (lifting) bridge over the harbour, rebuilt in 2018 with a dedicated cycling deck. Boats pass when the span tilts up.
stops.tip: Use the separated bike lane on the north side.

stops.name: Trackside Art Wall
stops.desc: Along the old rail corridor, the Trackside Art Wall is a rotating canvas of legal graffiti and murals — one of the city's best free art stops.
stops.tip: Great photo backdrop; the murals change often.

stops.name: Richards Island Viewpoint
stops.desc: Richards Island viewpoint looks out over the Gorge Waterway, a tidal inlet that was Victoria's first summer playground. Watch for herons, otters and paddlers.
stops.tip: Look for the current — the whole inlet is tidal.

stops.name: Gorgeous Gorge
stops.desc: At Tillicum Narrows the Gorge becomes one of the few reversing tidal falls in the world — the rapids literally flow backwards as the tide turns.
stops.tip: Time it with the tide change to catch the falls reversing.

stops.name: Bamfield Park Dock
stops.desc: Bamfield Park's little dock is a calm rest stop and a popular kayak launch tucked along the Gorge.
stops.tip: Good spot to stretch and refill before the trestle.

stops.name: Selkirk Trestle
stops.desc: The Selkirk Trestle is one of Canada's longest wooden rail trestles, now a walking-and-cycling bridge you ride right across the water.
stops.tip: Stop mid-span for the best view of the Gorge.

legs: Welcome to Gorge & The Goose — the longest loop at about 14 km, and almost all flat rail-trail. Roll out toward the harbour.
Approaching the Johnson Street Bridge — this bright-blue lifting bridge replaced a 1924 span that locals also called 'Big Blue'.
You're now on the Galloping Goose, a rail-trail built on a 1920s railway that once ran all the way to Sooke. The art wall is just ahead.
Riding along the Gorge Waterway — a century ago this tidal inlet was lined with swimming spots, boathouses and tea gardens.
Nearing Tillicum Narrows and the reversing falls — as the tide shifts, the saltwater rapids actually flow backward.
Cruising toward Bamfield Park's dock, a quiet pocket of waterfront and a favourite kayak launch.
Final landmark: the Selkirk Trestle, a huge restored wooden railway bridge you ride straight across before looping back.

qa.a: This loop is more nature than cafés. QV Cafe at the start/finish is your best bet; there are occasional kiosks near the Selkirk Trestle and Galloping Goose. Pack a snack for the long middle stretch.
Public washrooms are at Gorge Park (near the reversing falls) and around the Selkirk Trestle / Galloping Goose trailhead.
The Gorge Waterway is full of life — great blue herons, river otters, kingfishers and the occasional seal that swims up the inlet on the tide.
The Galloping Goose is a rail-trail on a 1920s railway line to Sooke; its quirky name came from a noisy gas-powered passenger car. The Selkirk Trestle is a restored wooden railway bridge.
This is the longest loop — about 14 km on flat, paved rail-trail. Plan for 1.5 to 2.5 hours with stops; faster riders under 1.5.
It's almost entirely flat and paved — the Galloping Goose is a former rail line, so grades are gentle. Easiest terrain of the three tours, just the longest.
The Gorge is more sheltered than the open coast, so it's a good pick on windy days. Still bring a light layer — the trestle crossings can be breezy.
Quick Zoomr rules: helmets are mandatory, top speed is 25 km/h, single rider only, and you ride in bike lanes or as far right as practical — no sidewalks. Minimum age 16.
Zoomrs start and end at a Zoomr Zone hub (the pilot hub is at QV Cafe & Bakery). Lock to the rack when you're done — ending a ride outside a Zone adds fees.

tours.blurb: The long, leafy one. Follow the Galloping Goose rail-trail and the tidal Gorge waterway past murals, herons and a historic wooden trestle.

367.text: Turn right and cross tillicum bridge

432.text: Turn left ahead to see selkirk trestle

added.text: Turn left and cross craigflower bridge
Turn left to head towards the pier
Bear left to cross Johnson Street Bridge

stops.name: QV Cafe & Bakery - Zoomr Zone
stops.desc: A ~7.6 km roll through Victoria's grandest heritage architecture — château hotels, parliament, and two genuine castles. Easy rolling pace.
stops.tip: Some stops (castle, parliament) have paid tours — check hours if you want to go inside.
stops.depart: When you are ready, head left down Government.

stops.name: Fairmont Empress
stops.desc: The Fairmont Empress is Victoria's ivy-draped landmark, opened in 1908 and famous for its afternoon tea served to royalty and celebrities alike.
stops.tip: Best photo is from the Inner Harbour causeway looking up.
stops.depart: Take in the sights and continue down government when you're ready.

stops.name: Legislative Assembly
stops.desc: The Legislative Assembly (Parliament Buildings) opened in 1898, designed by 25-year-old architect Francis Rattenbury. Over 3,300 lights outline it at night.
stops.tip: Free guided tours run inside on weekdays.
stops.depart: After this stop, lets continue down government towards beacon hill park.

stops.name: Stone Bridge
stops.desc: This picturesque stone bridge arches over the ponds of Beacon Hill Park, laid out in the 1880s in English garden style — a favourite with ducks and photographers.
stops.tip: Classic photo spot; watch for turtles on the logs in summer.
stops.depart: Take in the sights and continue across the stone bridge.

stops.name: Government House
stops.desc: Government House is the official residence of BC's Lieutenant Governor. The heritage rose gardens and grounds are open to the public, free of charge.
stops.tip: The gardens are free to wander — leave your Zoomr at the rack.
stops.depart: Lets head north to Craigdarroch Castle when you're ready.

stops.name: Craigdarroch Castle
stops.desc: Craigdarroch Castle is a 39-room stone mansion built in 1890 by coal magnate Robert Dunsmuir. Its woodwork and stained glass are some of the finest in North America.
stops.tip: Worth a tour inside for the stained glass and the tower view.
stops.depart: When you are done, head north west up to Fort Street to head back to the Zoomr Zone.

legs: Welcome to Cobble & Castles — about 7.6 km through Victoria's most photogenic heritage architecture. Easy rolling start.
Approaching the Fairmont Empress — opened in 1908 and covered in Boston ivy, it has served afternoon tea to kings, queens and movie stars.
The Parliament Buildings ahead were designed by a 25-year-old who won an anonymous competition. At night more than 3,300 bulbs light the facade.
Heading into Beacon Hill Park toward its stone bridge — the ponds and pathways were laid out in the 1880s in classic English-garden style.
Rolling up to Government House, home to BC's Lieutenant Governor; its rose gardens are open to everyone, free of charge.
The grand finale: Craigdarroch Castle, a 39-room Victorian 'bonanza castle' built by a coal baron in 1890.

qa.a: You're near downtown the whole way — endless options around the Inner Harbour and Fort Street. QV Cafe anchors the start/finish, and there are cafés steps from the Empress and Craigdarroch.
Washrooms are at the Inner Harbour causeway, inside Beacon Hill Park, and at the visitor facilities by Craigdarroch Castle.

This route is a tour through Victoria's Gilded Age: the Empress (1908) and Parliament (1898) were both designed by architect Francis Rattenbury, and Craigdarroch Castle (1890) was built by coal baron Robert Dunsmuir.
Craigdarroch Castle and (sometimes) the Parliament Buildings charge or require booking to go inside. Government House gardens and the park are free. Check current hours before you arrive.
About 7.6 km at an easy pace — roughly 1.5 hours with photo stops, or under an hour riding straight through. Add time if you tour the castle.
Mostly sheltered, urban streets and park paths, so it handles wind and light rain well. Victoria is mild year-round — a light layer is usually enough.
Quick Zoomr rules: helmets are mandatory, top speed is 25 km/h, single rider only, and you ride in bike lanes or as far right as practical — no sidewalks. Minimum age 16.
Zoomrs start and end at a Zoomr Zone hub (the pilot hub is at QV Cafe & Bakery). Lock to the rack when you're done — ending a ride outside a Zone adds fees.

tours.blurb: A heritage cruise through Victoria's grandest architecture — the Empress, the Parliament Buildings, and two genuine Victorian castles.
